headline: RM6.2B Tun Razak Exchange Fills Only 52% After 10 Years
context: The Tun Razak Exchange financial district in KL achieved only 52% occupancy a decade after launch, with the 70-acre development struggling to attract tenants despite RM6.2 billion in infrastructure investment and tax incentives.

cards.big: 52% occupied after 10 years — RM6.2 billion TRX financial district is half-empty while KL office oversupply hits 32%
cards.sub: Overall KL office vacancy rate at 32% — TRX added 8.5 million sq ft of premium space to a glutted market

cards.big: TRX tenants receive 10-year tax holidays and 100% investment tax allowance — subsidising rent competition against existing KL offices
cards.sub: Government incentives effectively cannibalise existing office districts — KLCC and Bangsar South vacancy rates rose 8% since TRX opened

cards.big: TRX land was originally a 1MDB asset — purchased at RM230 per sq ft and developed into luxury real estate
cards.sub: Public land turned into premium property benefits developers and tenants — not the broader Malaysian public who bore 1MDB losses

cards.big: TRX's Special Financial Zone status was approved without competitive assessment against other potential locations
cards.sub: No cost-benefit analysis comparing TRX development against upgrading existing financial districts was publicly released

cards.big: Building a new financial district in a city with 32% office vacancy isn't visionary — it's adding supply to a market already drowning in empty space
Should TRX tax incentives be phased out over 3 years to level the playing field with existing KL commercial districts?
